Inclusive Growth and Issues Arising from.title: Inclusive Growth and Issues Arising from It
Inclusive Growth and Issues Arising from.desc: Inclusive Growth refers to economic growth that is broad-based across sectors, creates productive employment, and reduces inequalities in income, wealth, and opportunities — a concept central to India's planning philosophy since the Eleventh Five Year Plan (2007–12) which adopted 'Faster and More Inclusive Growth' as its theme. It encompasses issues of poverty alleviation, social sector spending, financial inclusion, regional disparities, and equitable access to education, healthcare, and digital infrastructure. UPSC aspirants must master this topic as it cuts across economics, governance, and social justice dimensions, featuring prominently in GS Papers II and III.
facts: The Eleventh Five Year Plan (2007–12) formally adopted inclusive growth as India's development paradigm, targeting 9% GDP growth with emphasis on agriculture, education, health, and rural infrastructure. The Tendulkar Committee (2009) redefined the poverty line at Rs 816 per capita per month (rural) and Rs 1,000 (urban), while the Rangarajan Committee (2014) revised it upward, estimating 29.5% of the population as below the poverty line.

Financial inclusion received a massive impetus through the Pradhan Mantri Jan Dhan Yojana (PMJDY) launched on 28 August 2014, which opened over 52 crore bank accounts with zero balance facility, RuPay debit cards, and insurance cover. The JAM Trinity (Jan Dhan-Aadhaar-Mobile) framework enabled direct benefit transfers worth over Rs 30 lakh crore, reducing leakages estimated at 40% by the erstwhile Planning Commission.

India's Gini coefficient stood at approximately 0.35 (consumption-based), indicating moderate inequality, though wealth inequality is significantly higher with the top 10% holding over 65% of total wealth. The Multidimensional Poverty Index (MPI) by NITI Aayog (2023) showed that 135 million people exited multidimensional poverty between 2015–16 and 2019–21, with significant improvements in nutrition, sanitation, and cooking fuel.

The Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act (MGNREGA), 2005 guarantees 100 days of wage employment per rural household, serving as a critical social safety net. Despite criticism of implementation gaps, MGNREGA has enhanced rural purchasing power, reduced distress migration, and empowered women who constitute over 50% of the workforce in several states.

Regional disparities remain a significant challenge, with the per capita income of states like Goa and Delhi being 5–6 times that of Bihar and Uttar Pradesh. The Aspirational Districts Programme launched in 2018 targets 112 underdeveloped districts across governance, health, education, agriculture, and infrastructure, using a competitive, data-driven approach to bridge developmental gaps.
related: Indian Economy and Issues Relating to Growth, Development and Employment

India's Defence Modernization.title: India's Defence Modernization
India's Defence Modernization.desc: India's Defence Modernization encompasses the strategic overhaul of the armed forces through indigenous development and acquisition of advanced weaponry, platforms, and technologies under the broader framework of Atmanirbhar Bharat (self-reliant India) in defence. Governed by the Defence Acquisition Procedure (DAP) 2020, the Department of Military Affairs (DMA) established in 2020, and guided by the Chief of Defence Staff (CDS), modernization spans across the Army, Navy, Air Force, and emerging domains of cyber, space, and AI. UPSC aspirants must understand this topic for questions on national security, defence procurement, indigenous manufacturing, and India's strategic capabilities.
facts: The post of Chief of Defence Staff (CDS) was created on 1 January 2020 following the recommendation of the Kargil Review Committee (1999) and the Naresh Chandra Committee (2012), with General Bipin Rawat as the first CDS. The CDS heads the Department of Military Affairs (DMA) and serves as permanent chairman of the Chiefs of Staff Committee, promoting tri-service integration and jointness.

The Defence Acquisition Procedure (DAP) 2020 replaced DPP 2016 and introduced the 'Buy (Indian-IDDM)' category as the highest priority, mandating indigenous content of at least 50%. The positive indigenisation lists covering over 500 items impose progressive import bans, pushing the defence industry toward self-reliance with a target of Rs 1.75 lakh crore defence production by 2025.

India's defence budget for 2024–25 stood at approximately Rs 6.21 lakh crore (around 1.9% of GDP), with a significant shift toward capital expenditure for modernization. India is the world's largest arms importer according to SIPRI, though defence exports have risen tenfold to over Rs 21,000 crore in 2023–24, reflecting growing indigenous capabilities through platforms like BrahMos missiles, Tejas LCA, and INS Vikrant.

The establishment of two Defence Industrial Corridors in Uttar Pradesh (Lucknow-Kanpur-Jhansi) and Tamil Nadu (Chennai-Coimbatore-Salem) aims to develop a robust domestic defence manufacturing ecosystem. Over 100 startups have been funded through the iDEX (Innovations for Defence Excellence) initiative, fostering innovation in areas like AI, drones, cyber security, and advanced materials.

Key ongoing modernization programmes include the Project 75(I) for six advanced conventional submarines with Air Independent Propulsion, the Multi-Role Fighter Aircraft (MRFA) procurement for 114 jets, and the development of the Advanced Medium Combat Aircraft (AMCA) — India's fifth-generation stealth fighter. The Strategic Partnership (SP) Model under DAP engages Indian private sector companies as lead integrators with foreign OEMs for critical platforms.
related: Defence Procurement Procedure and Atmanirbhar Bharat in Defence

India's Energy Security.title: India's Energy Security
India's Energy Security.desc: India's Energy Security refers to the country's ability to ensure uninterrupted availability of energy sources at affordable prices to sustain economic growth and development, given that India imports approximately 85% of its crude oil and 50% of its natural gas requirements. The policy framework encompasses the Integrated Energy Policy (2006), the National Energy Policy draft by NITI Aayog, diversification of energy sources and suppliers, strategic petroleum reserves, and the ambitious renewable energy targets under the Paris Agreement. UPSC aspirants must master this topic as it intersects with economic growth, foreign policy, environmental sustainability, and geopolitical strategy.
facts: India is the world's third-largest energy consumer and third-largest crude oil importer, spending over $120 billion annually on energy imports which significantly impacts the current account deficit. The country's energy import dependence for crude oil stands at approximately 85%, making supply disruptions due to geopolitical conflicts (Gulf region, Russia-Ukraine) a critical vulnerability for economic stability.

India's Strategic Petroleum Reserves (SPR) at three locations — Visakhapatnam (1.33 MMT), Mangalore (1.5 MMT), and Padur (2.5 MMT) — provide approximately 9.5 days of import cover. Phase II expansion at Chandikhol (Odisha) and Padur (additional capacity) aims to increase reserves to 22 days of import cover, managed by the Indian Strategic Petroleum Reserves Limited (ISPRL).

The government's target of 500 GW non-fossil fuel installed capacity by 2030 and achieving 50% cumulative electric power from renewable sources forms a cornerstone of energy security strategy. India's total renewable energy capacity exceeded 190 GW by 2024, including solar (over 80 GW) and wind (over 46 GW), supported by schemes like the National Solar Mission and Production Linked Incentives for solar module manufacturing.

The National Green Hydrogen Mission, launched in January 2023 with an outlay of Rs 19,744 crore, targets production of 5 MMT of green hydrogen annually by 2030, positioning India as a global hub for green hydrogen production and export. This is complemented by the ethanol blending programme achieving 12% blending ahead of schedule, targeting 20% by 2025–26 to reduce oil import dependence.

India has actively diversified its energy supply sources through long-term LNG contracts with Qatar, Australia, and the US, equity investments in overseas oil and gas assets through OVL (ONGC Videsh Limited) in Russia, Mozambique, and Central Asia, and participation in the International Solar Alliance (ISA) co-founded with France in 2015 to promote solar energy deployment across 121 countries.

India's Industrial Policy.title: India's Industrial Policy
India's Industrial Policy.desc: India's Industrial Policy traces its evolution from the socialist-oriented Industrial Policy Resolutions of 1948 and 1956 through the liberalization watershed of the New Industrial Policy 1991 to the contemporary 'Make in India' and 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' frameworks that emphasize manufacturing competitiveness, ease of doing business, and global supply chain integration. The policy framework includes sector-specific strategies, Production Linked Incentive (PLI) schemes, industrial corridors, Special Economic Zones, and MSME support through schemes like MUDRA. UPSC aspirants must understand this topic as it underpins questions on economic reforms, industrialization patterns, employment generation, and India's aspiration to become a $5 trillion economy.
facts: The Industrial Policy Resolution 1956, inspired by the Mahalanobis Model and the socialistic pattern of society, classified industries into three schedules with the state dominating heavy and strategic industries. The New Industrial Policy 1991 dismantled the License Raj by abolishing industrial licensing for all but 18 industries (now reduced to 4), liberalizing FDI, and reforming public sector undertakings through disinvestment.

The Make in India initiative launched on 25 September 2014 targeted 25 sectors including automobiles, defence, electronics, and textiles to transform India into a global manufacturing hub. India's manufacturing sector contributes approximately 17% of GDP, with the National Manufacturing Policy (2011) and Make in India aiming to raise this to 25% and create 100 million additional jobs.

The Production Linked Incentive (PLI) Scheme, introduced in 2020 across 14 key sectors with an outlay of Rs 1.97 lakh crore over five years, offers financial incentives of 4–6% on incremental sales to boost domestic manufacturing. The mobile phone manufacturing PLI has been particularly successful, with India becoming the world's second-largest mobile manufacturer and Apple expanding production significantly.

The MSME sector, redefined under the Atmanirbhar Bharat package (2020) with revised investment and turnover criteria, contributes approximately 30% to GDP and 45% to total exports. The MUDRA Yojana (2015) under PMMY has disbursed over Rs 23 lakh crore in collateral-free loans to micro-enterprises, with over 70% of beneficiaries being women entrepreneurs.

India's industrial corridor programme includes the Delhi-Mumbai Industrial Corridor (DMIC), Amritsar-Kolkata Industrial Corridor (AKIC), Chennai-Bengaluru Industrial Corridor (CBIC), and others totalling 11 corridors spanning 17 states. The National Industrial Corridor Development Programme under DPIIT aims to develop greenfield smart cities as integrated manufacturing hubs with world-class infrastructure, logistics, and connectivity.

India's Nationally Determined Contributi.title: India's Nationally Determined Contributions
India's Nationally Determined Contributi.desc: India's Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) represent the country's commitments under the Paris Agreement (2015) to combat climate change, updated in August 2022 to reflect enhanced ambitions including reducing carbon intensity of GDP by 45% by 2030 (from 2005 levels) and achieving 50% cumulative electric power from non-fossil fuel sources. Rooted in the principle of Common But Differentiated Responsibilities (CBDR) and equity, India's NDCs balance developmental imperatives with climate obligations while pursuing the net-zero target of 2070 announced at COP26 in Glasgow. UPSC aspirants must understand NDCs for questions on environment, international agreements, sustainable development, and India's climate diplomacy.
facts: India's updated NDCs (August 2022) enhanced two key targets: reducing the emissions intensity of GDP by 45% by 2030 compared to 2005 levels (up from 33–35% in the original 2016 NDCs), and achieving 50% cumulative installed electric power capacity from non-fossil fuel sources by 2030 (up from 40%). These updates formalized the 'Panchamrit' commitments announced by PM Modi at COP26 Glasgow (November 2021).

At COP26, India announced its net-zero emissions target by 2070, making it one of the last major economies to set such a target. The five 'Panchamrit' elements include reaching 500 GW non-fossil fuel capacity by 2030, meeting 50% energy needs from renewables, reducing total projected carbon emissions by 1 billion tonnes by 2030, and reducing carbon intensity by 45%.

India's per capita emissions at approximately 1.9 tonnes CO2 are well below the global average of 4.7 tonnes and significantly lower than the US (14.4 tonnes) and China (8.0 tonnes). India has consistently invoked the principle of Common But Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities (CBDR-RC) under Article 3 of the UNFCCC to argue that developed nations must take the lead in emission reductions and provide climate finance.

The National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC), launched in 2008, comprises eight national missions including the National Solar Mission, National Mission for Enhanced Energy Efficiency, National Water Mission, and National Mission for a Green India. These missions provide the domestic policy framework for achieving NDC targets through sector-specific strategies and state-level action plans.

India has been a leading voice in demanding climate finance of $100 billion annually from developed nations (as promised at COP15 Copenhagen, 2009, and largely unmet) and pushed for the New Collective Quantified Goal (NCQG) at COP29. The Loss and Damage Fund operationalized at COP28 Dubai (2023) represented a significant diplomatic achievement for developing nations including India.
related: India's net-zero commitments and fossil fuel transition

India's bilateral relations with develop.title: India's Bilateral Relations with Developed Nations
India's bilateral relations with develop.desc: India's bilateral relations with developed nations — including the United States, European Union members, Japan, Australia, the United Kingdom, France, and Germany — have undergone a strategic transformation in the post-Cold War era, evolving from non-alignment to multi-alignment with comprehensive partnerships spanning trade, defence, technology, and climate cooperation. These relationships are anchored in institutional frameworks such as 2+2 Ministerial Dialogues, strategic partnerships, and multilateral coordination in forums like the Quad, G20, and UNSC reform negotiations. UPSC aspirants must understand these relationships as they are essential for questions on India's foreign policy, strategic partnerships, trade agreements, defence cooperation, and the evolving global order.
facts: The India-US relationship, elevated to a 'Comprehensive Global Strategic Partnership' in 2020, is anchored by the 2+2 Ministerial Dialogue (since 2018), four foundational defence agreements — LEMOA (2016), COMCASA (2018), BECA (2020), and GSOMIA — and the Initiative on Critical and Emerging Technology (iCET) launched in 2023 covering semiconductors, AI, quantum computing, and space.

India and Japan share a 'Special Strategic and Global Partnership' since 2014, with Japan being a key partner in India's infrastructure development through projects like the Mumbai-Ahmedabad High Speed Rail (bullet train) corridor, the Delhi-Mumbai Industrial Corridor, and development of India's northeastern region. The Acquisition and Cross-Servicing Agreement (ACSA) signed in 2020 deepened defence logistics cooperation.

The India-France strategic partnership, initiated in 1998, is characterized by convergence on strategic autonomy, Indo-Pacific cooperation, and defence ties including the Rafale fighter jet deal (36 aircraft) and Scorpene submarine technology transfer under Project 75. France supports India's permanent membership in the UN Security Council and has been a reliable partner in nuclear energy cooperation.

India's relations with Australia were elevated to a 'Comprehensive Strategic Partnership' in 2020, with the Quad (India-US-Japan-Australia) serving as a key multilateral framework for Indo-Pacific security and economic cooperation. The India-Australia Economic Cooperation and Trade Agreement (ECTA), signed in April 2022, eliminated tariffs on 85% of Australian goods and 96% of Indian goods, marking India's first trade deal with a developed economy in over a decade.

India-EU relations, designated a Strategic Partnership since 2004, were reinvigorated through the India-EU Trade and Technology Council (TTC) launched in 2023 — only the second such mechanism after the US-EU TTC. Negotiations on the India-EU Broad Based Trade and Investment Agreement (BTIA), stalled since 2013, were relaunched in 2022, covering goods, services, investment protection, and digital trade provisions.

India's net-zero commitments and fossil.title: India's Net-Zero Commitments and Fossil Fuel Transition
India's net-zero commitments and fossil.desc: India committed to achieving net-zero carbon emissions by 2070 at COP26 in Glasgow (2021), alongside interim targets of 500 GW non-fossil fuel capacity and 50% renewable energy share by 2030. This topic is grounded in India's Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) under the Paris Agreement and the Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act, 2022, which introduced a carbon credit trading scheme. UPSC aspirants must understand this as it intersects environment, energy security, international negotiations, and India's development imperatives.
facts: At COP26 (Glasgow, 2021), Prime Minister Modi announced India's 'Panchamrit' targets, including reaching 500 GW non-fossil fuel energy capacity by 2030 and achieving net-zero emissions by 2070, making India the last major economy to set a net-zero target.

The Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act, 2022 provides the legal framework for a domestic carbon credit trading scheme and mandates minimum use of non-fossil fuel sources by designated consumers, strengthening India's regulatory infrastructure for the energy transition.

India's National Solar Mission under the National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC, 2008) has been revised upward to 100 GW solar capacity by 2022 (later extended), and India became the third-largest solar power producer globally by 2023.

The International Solar Alliance (ISA), co-founded by India and France in 2015 and headquartered in Gurugram, became the first treaty-based international organization headquartered in India, reflecting India's leadership in renewable energy diplomacy.

India faces the challenge of coal dependency — coal still accounts for over 70% of electricity generation — and the concept of 'just transition' is critical as millions of livelihoods in coal-bearing states like Jharkhand and Chhattisgarh depend on the fossil fuel economy.
related: Energy security and diversification of imports

India's position on the Russia-Ukraine c.title: India's Position on the Russia-Ukraine Conflict
India's position on the Russia-Ukraine c.desc: India has maintained a position of strategic neutrality on the Russia-Ukraine conflict that began in February 2022, abstaining from multiple UN resolutions condemning Russia while calling for dialogue and diplomacy. This stance is rooted in India's longstanding doctrine of strategic autonomy under Article 73 of the Constitution (executive power in foreign affairs) and its non-aligned heritage. UPSC aspirants must study this as it illustrates India's balancing act between its defence ties with Russia, economic partnerships with the West, and its aspirations for a multipolar world order.
facts: India abstained from key UNSC and UNGA resolutions on the Russia-Ukraine conflict in 2022-2023, including the March 2022 UNGA resolution (ES-11/1) demanding Russian withdrawal, reflecting its policy of strategic autonomy rather than alignment with either bloc.

India's imports of discounted Russian crude oil surged from under 1% to over 20% of total crude imports after the conflict began, highlighting the tension between Western sanctions regimes and India's energy security imperatives as the world's third-largest oil importer.

Prime Minister Modi's statement to President Putin that 'today's era is not of war' at the Samarkand SCO Summit (September 2022) became a landmark diplomatic articulation, later endorsed by the G20 New Delhi Declaration (2023) which India shaped as G20 President.

India's position is shaped by its decades-long defence dependence on Russia — over 60% of Indian military equipment is of Russian origin — making an adversarial stance strategically costly, especially given ongoing deliveries of the S-400 missile defence system.

The conflict has tested India's multilateral diplomacy, as India leveraged its G20 Presidency (2023) to bridge divides, successfully negotiating consensus language on the war and demonstrating its capacity as a 'bridge power' in a fractured global order.

India-U.S. Bilateral Relations.title: India-U.S. Bilateral Relations
India-U.S. Bilateral Relations.desc: India-U.S. bilateral relations have evolved from Cold War estrangement to a comprehensive global strategic partnership, formalized through the 2020 Comprehensive Global Strategic Partnership and underpinned by shared democratic values and convergent Indo-Pacific interests. The relationship spans defence, trade, technology, and people-to-people ties, governed by executive agreements under Article 73 and various bilateral frameworks. UPSC aspirants must study this as it is central to understanding India's foreign policy, defence modernization, trade negotiations, and positioning in the evolving multipolar order.
facts: The India-U.S. Civil Nuclear Agreement (2008), also known as the 123 Agreement, marked a watershed moment by granting India access to civilian nuclear technology despite being a non-signatory to the NPT, fundamentally transforming the bilateral relationship.

India and the U.S. have signed four foundational defence agreements — LEMOA (2016), COMCASA (2018), BECA (2020), and the earlier GSOMIA (2002) — enabling interoperability, logistics sharing, encrypted communications, and geospatial intelligence cooperation.

The Initiative on Critical and Emerging Technologies (iCET), launched in January 2023, focuses on cooperation in AI, quantum computing, semiconductors, and space, reflecting the technology-driven transformation of the partnership beyond traditional defence ties.

Bilateral trade between India and the U.S. exceeded $190 billion in 2023-24, making the U.S. India's largest trading partner, though persistent issues include market access barriers, IPR concerns, and the withdrawal of India's GSP (Generalized System of Preferences) benefits in 2019.

The Indian diaspora in the U.S., numbering over 4.4 million, is the highest-earning ethnic group in America and plays a pivotal role in strengthening bilateral ties through H-1B visa holders, political participation, and lobbying — exemplified by high-profile leaders in U.S. politics and corporate America.

India-US Bilateral Relations.title: India-US Bilateral Relations: Strategic and Diplomatic Dimensions
India-US Bilateral Relations.desc: India-US bilateral relations represent a defining partnership of the 21st century, characterized by convergence on counter-terrorism, maritime security in the Indo-Pacific, and a rules-based international order. The relationship operates through institutionalized mechanisms like the 2+2 Ministerial Dialogue and the Quad framework. UPSC aspirants must understand the strategic, economic, and diplomatic dimensions of this relationship as it features prominently in GS Paper II (International Relations) and GS Paper III (Security).
facts: The 2+2 Ministerial Dialogue, inaugurated in 2018, brings together the Foreign and Defence Ministers of both countries and has become the highest-level institutional mechanism for discussing strategic, defence, and security issues in the bilateral relationship.

The Quadrilateral Security Dialogue (Quad), comprising India, U.S., Japan, and Australia, was elevated to the Leaders' Summit level in 2021, reflecting shared concerns over China's assertiveness in the Indo-Pacific and cooperation on vaccines, climate, and critical technologies.

India was designated a Major Defence Partner by the U.S. in 2016 and subsequently granted Strategic Trade Authorization tier-1 (STA-1) status in 2018, placing India on par with NATO allies for access to advanced defence and dual-use technologies.

The U.S.-India Defence Technology and Trade Initiative (DTTI), launched in 2012, aims to move beyond a buyer-seller relationship toward co-development and co-production of defence equipment, with projects including jet engine technology transfer under the GE-HAL F414 engine deal (2023).

Key areas of friction include India's purchase of the Russian S-400 system potentially triggering CAATSA sanctions, disagreements over data localization policies, India's digital services tax, and differing positions on human rights and democratic governance issues.

India-US bilateral relations and trade d.title: India-US Bilateral Relations and Trade Dynamics
India-US bilateral relations and trade d.desc: India-US trade dynamics encompass a rapidly growing but often contentious commercial relationship, with bilateral goods and services trade exceeding $190 billion, marked by disputes over tariffs, market access, intellectual property, and trade deficits. The trade relationship is governed by WTO rules, bilateral trade policy frameworks, and the Foreign Trade (Development and Regulation) Act, 1992 on the Indian side. UPSC aspirants must study this for GS Paper II (bilateral relations) and GS Paper III (external sector, trade policy), as trade is increasingly central to the strategic partnership.
facts: The U.S. revoked India's GSP (Generalized System of Preferences) benefits in June 2019, affecting approximately $6.3 billion worth of Indian exports that had enjoyed duty-free access, citing India's failure to provide equitable and reasonable market access to U.S. commerce.

India's trade surplus with the U.S. has been a persistent irritant — India exported goods worth over $77 billion to the U.S. in 2023-24 while importing approximately $50 billion, with the U.S. pushing for greater market access in agriculture, dairy, medical devices, and ICT sectors.

The Trade Policy Forum (TPF), re-established in 2021 after a four-year hiatus, serves as the principal bilateral trade dialogue mechanism with working groups on agriculture, investment, IPR, services, and tariff/non-tariff barriers.

India's retaliatory tariffs on 28 U.S. products imposed in June 2019, including almonds, apples, and walnuts, and the U.S. concerns over India's high applied tariff rates (average bound tariff of 48.5% on agricultural products) remain key friction points in bilateral trade negotiations.

The services trade dimension is crucial — India runs a services trade surplus with the U.S., driven by IT/BPO exports exceeding $30 billion annually, while U.S. concerns focus on India's data localization requirements under the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023.

India-US economic relations and trade re.title: India-US Economic Relations and Trade Rebalancing
India-US economic relations and trade re.desc: India-US economic relations and trade rebalancing refer to ongoing efforts to address structural asymmetries in bilateral trade, including tariff differentials, market access barriers, and investment flows, while deepening economic integration in strategic sectors like semiconductors, clean energy, and supply chain resilience. These efforts are framed within the broader context of de-risking global supply chains from China dependence. UPSC aspirants must understand this topic as it connects trade policy, industrial strategy, and geopolitical realignment — critical for GS Paper II and III.
facts: The U.S.-India Supply Chain Resilience Initiative and participation in the Indo-Pacific Economic Framework (IPEF) launched in May 2022 reflect efforts to create alternative supply chains reducing dependence on China in sectors like semiconductors, pharmaceuticals, and rare earth minerals.

India's Production Linked Incentive (PLI) schemes across 14 sectors with an outlay of Rs 1.97 lakh crore have attracted U.S. investment, particularly in electronics manufacturing, with companies like Apple expanding iPhone production in India through contract manufacturers.

The U.S.-India Commercial Dialogue and CEO Forum serve as platforms for private sector engagement, with U.S. FDI in India exceeding $55 billion (2000-2024), concentrated in services, computer software, and pharmaceuticals sectors.

Trade rebalancing efforts include India's increased imports of U.S. crude oil and LNG — the U.S. became a top-5 crude oil supplier to India — and defence purchases exceeding $20 billion since 2007, including C-17 Globemasters, Apache helicopters, and MH-60R Seahawk helicopters.

The India-U.S. semiconductor partnership under iCET has led to Micron Technology's $2.75 billion semiconductor assembly facility in Gujarat (2023) and collaboration on chip design ecosystems, reflecting the strategic imperative of technology sovereignty and supply chain security.

Indian Economy and Issues Relating to Gr.title: Indian Economy and Issues Relating to Growth, Development and Employment
Indian Economy and Issues Relating to Gr.desc: This foundational UPSC topic covers the macroeconomic performance of the Indian economy, structural challenges in achieving inclusive growth, and persistent employment concerns including jobless growth, informal sector dominance, and skill mismatches. It draws on constitutional provisions under the Directive Principles (Articles 38, 39, 41, 43), FRBM Act 2003, and institutional frameworks like NITI Aayog. UPSC aspirants must master this as it forms the core of GS Paper III and intersects with governance, social justice, and development questions across all papers.
facts: India became the world's fifth-largest economy in 2022, surpassing the UK with a GDP exceeding $3.5 trillion, yet its per capita income remains around $2,500, reflecting the challenge of translating aggregate growth into individual prosperity and inclusive development.

The Periodic Labour Force Survey (PLFS) 2022-23 reported India's unemployment rate at 3.2% (usual status), but this masks structural issues — over 80% of employment is in the informal sector, with low wages and no social security, and youth unemployment (15-29 years) remains significantly higher at around 10%.

The K-shaped recovery post-COVID highlighted growing inequality — Oxfam's India Inequality Report (2022) noted the top 10% holds 77% of national wealth, while MGNREGA demand surged to over 300 crore person-days in 2020-21, reflecting rural distress.

India's demographic dividend — with 65% of the population below 35 years — presents both opportunity and challenge; the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 and Skill India Mission aim to address employability gaps, but the economy needs to create 12 million jobs annually to absorb new entrants.

The twin balance sheet problem of stressed corporate balance sheets and bank NPAs constrained growth post-2015; reforms including the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (IBC), 2016 and bank recapitalization have improved credit flow, with gross NPAs declining from 11.2% (2018) to around 3% (2024).
related: Inclusive Growth and Issues Arising from It

Indo-Pacific Oceans Initiative and marit.title: Indo-Pacific Oceans Initiative (IPOI) and Maritime Domain Awareness
Indo-Pacific Oceans Initiative and marit.desc: The Indo-Pacific Oceans Initiative (IPOI) was proposed by India at the East Asia Summit in 2019 as a cooperative framework for maritime security, ecology, disaster management, trade connectivity, and capacity building across the Indo-Pacific region. Maritime Domain Awareness (MDA) involves monitoring and understanding activities in the maritime domain for security and governance. UPSC aspirants must study this as it reflects India's proactive Indo-Pacific strategy, its SAGAR (Security and Growth for All in the Region) vision, and the growing importance of maritime security in GS Paper II and III.
facts: The IPOI was launched by PM Modi at the 14th East Asia Summit in Bangkok (November 2019) with seven pillars: maritime security, maritime ecology, maritime resources, capacity building and resource sharing, disaster risk reduction, science and technology cooperation, and trade connectivity and maritime transport.

India's Information Fusion Centre – Indian Ocean Region (IFC-IOR), established in Gurugram in 2018, serves as the nodal centre for maritime domain awareness, sharing shipping data and intelligence with over 25 partner nations and multinational organizations to track vessels and identify threats.

The SAGAR (Security and Growth for All in the Region) doctrine, articulated by PM Modi in March 2015, provides the strategic vision underlying IPOI, emphasizing India's role as a net security provider in the Indian Ocean and its commitment to cooperative maritime governance.

India has deployed coastal surveillance radar systems in Mauritius, Seychelles, Maldives, and Sri Lanka and conducted Mission SAGAR I-V during COVID-19 to provide humanitarian aid, demonstrating capacity building and maritime diplomacy in the Indo-Pacific littoral states.

The Indian Navy's operational doctrine emphasizes 'Mission Based Deployments' across the Indian Ocean Region, maintaining simultaneous presence in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Malacca, and Mozambique Channel, while India's first National Maritime Security Coordinator (NMSC) was appointed in 2022 to ensure whole-of-government approach to maritime security.
related: Freedom of Navigation and Indo-Pacific maritime strategy
Bilateral, Regional and Global Groupings Involving India

Indo-Pacific strategy and economic diplo.title: Indo-Pacific Strategy and Economic Diplomacy
Indo-Pacific strategy and economic diplo.desc: India's Indo-Pacific strategy refers to its comprehensive approach to ensuring a free, open, inclusive, and rules-based order in the region stretching from the eastern coast of Africa to the western Pacific, integrating security, economic, and diplomatic instruments. Economic diplomacy in this context involves leveraging trade, investment, connectivity, and institutional frameworks like the Quad and IPEF to advance strategic interests. UPSC aspirants must study this as it is central to India's foreign policy, figuring prominently in GS Paper II (International Relations) and representing India's response to China's Belt and Road Initiative.
facts: PM Modi articulated India's Indo-Pacific vision at the Shangri-La Dialogue (June 2018), defining the Indo-Pacific as stretching 'from the shores of Africa to that of the Americas' and emphasizing inclusiveness, ASEAN centrality, and rejection of a 'club of limited members' — distinguishing India's approach from the U.S. strategy.

The Indo-Pacific Economic Framework (IPEF), launched in May 2022 with 14 member countries representing 40% of global GDP, covers four pillars — trade, supply chains, clean economy, and fair economy — though India opted out of the trade pillar due to concerns over labour, environment, and digital trade commitments.

India's Act East Policy (upgraded from Look East in 2014) forms the economic backbone of its Indo-Pacific engagement, with ASEAN being India's fourth-largest trading partner and bilateral trade exceeding $130 billion, supported by the ASEAN-India Free Trade Agreement (2010).

The Asia-Africa Growth Corridor (AAGC), proposed jointly by India and Japan in 2017, and the India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC) announced at the G20 New Delhi Summit (2023) represent India's alternative connectivity visions to China's BRI, linking the Indo-Pacific with Europe and Africa.

India's Line of Credit diplomacy — extending over $30 billion in concessional credit to Indo-Pacific nations through EXIM Bank — and development partnerships in port construction (Chabahar, Sittwe), submarine cables, and digital connectivity represent the economic tools of India's Indo-Pacific strategy.

Industrial Corridor Development.title: Industrial Corridor Development
Industrial Corridor Development.desc: Industrial corridor development refers to India's strategy of creating planned industrial ecosystems along major transport routes, integrating world-class infrastructure, smart cities, logistics hubs, and manufacturing zones to drive economic growth and employment. The programme, anchored by the Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT) and the National Industrial Corridor Development Corporation (NICDC), draws on the National Manufacturing Policy 2011 and the Make in India initiative. UPSC aspirants must study this as it connects industrial policy, urbanization, infrastructure, and regional development — key topics in GS Paper III.
facts: The Delhi-Mumbai Industrial Corridor (DMIC), India's flagship industrial corridor spanning 1,504 km across six states, was launched with Japanese collaboration and a $100 billion investment vision, featuring smart cities like Dholera (Gujarat) and Vikram Udyogpuri (Ujjain) as industrial nodes.

India has planned 11 industrial corridors under the National Industrial Corridor Programme covering 17 states, including CBIC (Chennai-Bengaluru), AKIC (Amritsar-Kolkata), ECIC (East Coast), and VDIC (Vizag-Chennai-Hyderabad), designed to create a manufacturing and services ecosystem across the country.

The National Industrial Corridor Development Corporation (NICDC), set up as a Special Purpose Vehicle under DPIIT, acts as the apex body for coordinating development across all corridors, with trunk infrastructure funded through budgetary support, multilateral lending, and PPP models.

Industrial corridors are integrated with Dedicated Freight Corridors (DFCs) — the Western DFC (1,504 km) and Eastern DFC (1,856 km), both operationalized by 2024, which reduce logistics costs from 13-14% of GDP toward the target of 8% by enabling efficient freight movement.

The PM GatiShakti National Master Plan, launched in October 2021, provides a GIS-based digital platform integrating 16 ministries for coordinated planning of infrastructure and industrial corridor projects, addressing the historical challenge of siloed planning, land acquisition delays, and last-mile connectivity gaps.

Inflation and its socio-political impact.title: Inflation and Its Socio-Political Impact
Inflation and its socio-political impact.desc: Inflation refers to the sustained increase in the general price level, measured primarily by the Consumer Price Index (CPI) in India, and its socio-political impact encompasses effects on poverty, inequality, electoral outcomes, social unrest, and policy responses including monetary tightening by RBI under the inflation-targeting framework. The legal basis for inflation management lies in Section 45ZB of the RBI Act, 1934 (as amended in 2016), which established the Monetary Policy Committee with a 4% CPI inflation target (with +/- 2% band). UPSC aspirants must study this as inflation intersects economics (GS III), governance (GS II), and ethics (GS IV) through its disproportionate impact on the poor.
facts: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC), constituted under Section 45ZB of the RBI Act, 1934 through the Finance Act, 2016, has a statutory mandate to maintain CPI inflation at 4% (+/- 2%); failure to maintain this band for three consecutive quarters triggers an accountability mechanism requiring RBI to explain to the government.

The Urjit Patel Committee (2014) recommended the shift from WPI to CPI as the nominal anchor for monetary policy, recognizing that CPI better captures the inflation experienced by households, particularly the impact of food inflation which constitutes 46% of the CPI basket and disproportionately affects the poor.

Food inflation has been the primary driver of socio-political unrest in India — the 2013 onion price crisis contributed to the ruling party's defeat in state elections, illustrating how essential commodity price spikes directly influence electoral outcomes and trigger government interventions like export bans and stock limits.

Inflation acts as a regressive tax — studies show that a 1% increase in food inflation pushes approximately 2.5 crore people below the poverty line, widening inequality as the poor spend over 50% of their expenditure on food compared to 25-30% for higher income groups.

The MGNREGA wage linkage to CPI-Rural, DA (Dearness Allowance) indexation for government employees, and PM Garib Kalyan Anna Yojana represent policy responses to inflation's socio-political impact, while the Targeted Public Distribution System (TPDS) under the National Food Security Act, 2013 provides subsidized foodgrains to over 80 crore beneficiaries.

Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Ai.title: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Ai.desc: India's infrastructure development across energy, ports, roads, airports, and railways forms the backbone of economic growth, governed by sector-specific legislation including the Electricity Act 2003, Major Port Authorities Act 2021, National Highways Act 1956, and the Railways Act 1989, with overarching coordination through the PM GatiShakti National Master Plan and the National Infrastructure Pipeline (NIP). UPSC aspirants must comprehensively understand this topic as it spans GS Paper III (Infrastructure, Economic Development) and GS Paper II (Government Policies), intersecting with federalism, PPP models, and sustainable development.
facts: The National Infrastructure Pipeline (NIP), announced in 2019, envisions an investment of Rs 111 lakh crore ($1.5 trillion) across 7,400+ projects during 2020-2025 in sectors including energy, roads, railways, and urban infrastructure, with funding split between Centre (39%), states (39%), and private sector (22%).

India's road network — the second-largest globally at 6.4 million km — has expanded rapidly under Bharatmala Pariyojana (Phase I: 34,800 km at Rs 5.35 lakh crore), with National Highway construction reaching a record 12,162 km in 2022-23 and the target of building 2 lakh km of National Highways by 2025.

The Indian Railways, the fourth-largest rail network globally (68,000+ route km), is undergoing transformation through 100% electrification (completed over 90% by 2024), Dedicated Freight Corridors, the Vande Bharat Express programme, and investments exceeding Rs 2.5 lakh crore annually in the Union Budget.

India's port capacity has expanded significantly under the Sagarmala Programme (2015) with an investment of Rs 6 lakh crore across 800+ projects, while the Major Port Authorities Act, 2021 replaced the 1963 Act to grant greater autonomy to major ports and enable PPP-based development, increasing total port capacity to over 2,500 MTPA.

India's airport infrastructure has seen the number of operational airports increase from 74 (2014) to over 150 (2024) under the UDAN (Ude Desh ka Aam Naagrik) scheme for regional connectivity, while India became the third-largest domestic aviation market globally, with the Noida International Airport (Jewar) and Navi Mumbai Airport representing next-generation greenfield developments.

Innovation Ecosystem.title: Innovation Ecosystem
Innovation Ecosystem.desc: An innovation ecosystem refers to the interconnected network of institutions, policies, funding mechanisms, and human capital that collectively foster research, development, and commercialization of new technologies and ideas. India's innovation framework is governed by the Science and Technology Policy, the National Innovation Policy, and institutional mechanisms like Atal Innovation Mission, NITI Aayog, and DST. UPSC aspirants must understand this topic as it intersects with economic development, industrial policy, self-reliance under Atmanirbhar Bharat, and India's global competitiveness in the knowledge economy.
facts: Atal Innovation Mission (AIM) was launched in 2016 under NITI Aayog to create an umbrella structure for innovation and entrepreneurship across the country, establishing Atal Tinkering Labs in schools and Atal Incubation Centres for startups. It aims to foster a culture of innovation from grassroots to advanced research levels.

India's Global Innovation Index (GII) ranking has improved significantly, rising from 81st in 2015 to 39th in 2023, reflecting the growing maturity of the innovation ecosystem. This improvement is attributed to increased R&D spending, digital infrastructure expansion, and a thriving startup ecosystem with over 100 unicorns.

The National Research Foundation (NRF), established under the Anusandhan National Research Foundation Act, 2023, was created to seed, grow, and promote research across universities and colleges with a proposed outlay of Rs 50,000 crore over five years. It replaces the earlier Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB).

India's gross expenditure on Research and Development (R&D) has historically remained around 0.65-0.7% of GDP, significantly lower than the global average of about 1.8% and far below countries like Israel (4.9%) and South Korea (4.8%). The Science, Technology and Innovation Policy 2020 aims to increase this to 2% of GDP.

Startup India, launched on January 16, 2016, provides a comprehensive action plan offering tax exemptions under Section 80-IAC of the Income Tax Act, simplified compliance, and funding through the Fund of Funds for Startups (FFS) managed by SIDBI with a corpus of Rs 10,000 crore.

International Environmental Governance.title: International Environmental Governance
International Environmental Governance.desc: International Environmental Governance encompasses the multilateral treaties, institutions, and normative frameworks that regulate global environmental protection, including climate change, biodiversity loss, and pollution. Key frameworks include the UNFCCC, the Paris Agreement (2015), the Convention on Biological Diversity (1992), and institutions like UNEP established in 1972. UPSC aspirants must master this topic as it is critical for understanding India's Nationally Determined Contributions, the principle of Common But Differentiated Responsibilities (CBDR), climate justice, and India's negotiating positions at global environmental forums.
facts: The United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP), headquartered in Nairobi, Kenya, was established in 1972 following the Stockholm Conference on the Human Environment. It serves as the leading global environmental authority that sets the environmental agenda and promotes coherent implementation of the environmental dimension of sustainable development.

The Paris Agreement (2015) under the UNFCCC commits signatory nations to limit global temperature rise to well below 2°C above pre-industrial levels, with efforts to limit it to 1.5°C. India ratified it in 2016 and submitted enhanced NDCs in 2022, pledging to achieve 50% cumulative electric power from non-fossil fuel sources by 2030 and reach net-zero by 2070.

The Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD), signed at the Rio Earth Summit in 1992, has three main objectives: conservation of biological diversity, sustainable use of its components, and fair and equitable sharing of benefits from genetic resources. The Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (2022) set the target of protecting 30% of land and oceans by 2030 (30x30 target).

The principle of Common But Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities (CBDR-RC), enshrined in Principle 7 of the Rio Declaration (1992), acknowledges that developed countries bear greater responsibility for environmental degradation. India consistently invokes CBDR to argue for climate justice and adequate climate finance from developed nations.

The Green Climate Fund (GCF), established in 2010 under the UNFCCC framework, is the world's largest dedicated climate fund with a mandate to channel $100 billion annually from developed to developing nations for mitigation and adaptation. The COP29 in Baku (2024) agreed to a new collective quantified goal of $300 billion per year by 2035, though developing nations had demanded $1.3 trillion.

International Humanitarian Law and armed.title: International Humanitarian Law and Armed Conflicts
International Humanitarian Law and armed.desc: International Humanitarian Law (IHL), also known as the law of armed conflict or jus in bello, is a set of international rules that seek to limit the effects of armed conflict by protecting persons who are not or are no longer participating in hostilities and by restricting the means and methods of warfare. The principal instruments of IHL are the four Geneva Conventions of 1949 and their Additional Protocols of 1977 and 2005, administered by the International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC). UPSC aspirants must study IHL as it is essential for understanding India's obligations in conflict situations, its position on the Russia-Ukraine conflict, ethical dimensions of warfare, and its relevance to GS Paper II (International Relations) and GS Paper IV (Ethics).
facts: The four Geneva Conventions of 1949 form the core of IHL and have achieved universal ratification by all 196 states, making them among the most widely accepted international treaties. They protect wounded and sick soldiers on land (First Convention), shipwrecked military personnel at sea (Second), prisoners of war (Third), and civilians in time of war (Fourth Convention).

The Additional Protocol I (1977) extended protections to victims of international armed conflicts, while Additional Protocol II (1977) was the first treaty devoted exclusively to non-international armed conflicts. India has ratified the four Geneva Conventions but has not ratified the Additional Protocols, though it generally adheres to their customary law principles.

The International Criminal Court (ICC), established by the Rome Statute of 1998 and operational since 2002, prosecutes individuals for genocide, war crimes, crimes against humanity, and the crime of aggression. India is not a party to the Rome Statute, citing concerns over the ICC's jurisdiction potentially overriding national sovereignty and its provisions on the crime of aggression.

The principle of distinction requires parties to a conflict to distinguish between combatants and civilians at all times, while the principle of proportionality prohibits attacks that would cause civilian harm excessive in relation to the military advantage anticipated. These principles form the bedrock of IHL and are customary international law binding on all states.

The Martens Clause, first introduced in the Hague Convention of 1899, provides that in situations not covered by specific treaty provisions, civilians and combatants remain under the protection of principles of humanity and the dictates of public conscience. This clause has been cited by the International Court of Justice (ICJ) in its 1996 Advisory Opinion on the legality of nuclear weapons.

International Trade and WTO.title: International Trade and WTO
International Trade and WTO.desc: International trade involves the exchange of goods, services, and capital across borders, governed by a rules-based multilateral trading system anchored in the World Trade Organization (WTO), which replaced the GATT in 1995. The WTO administers trade agreements, provides a forum for negotiations, handles dispute settlement under the Dispute Settlement Understanding (DSU), and monitors national trade policies. UPSC aspirants must thoroughly understand this topic as it covers India's trade policy, the impact of globalization, free trade agreements, trade disputes, and their implications for Indian agriculture, industry, and services sectors.
facts: The World Trade Organization (WTO) was established on January 1, 1995, succeeding the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT, 1947), following the Uruguay Round of negotiations (1986-1994). It is headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland, and has 164 member states as of 2024, with its highest decision-making body being the Ministerial Conference held every two years.

The WTO Dispute Settlement Body (DSB) operates through the Dispute Settlement Understanding (DSU) and includes an Appellate Body that has been non-functional since December 2019 due to the United States blocking new appointments. India has been involved in significant disputes, including the India-Solar Cells case (DS456) where India's domestic content requirements under the National Solar Mission were ruled inconsistent with WTO obligations.

The Agreement on Agriculture (AoA) under the WTO is built on three pillars: market access, domestic support, and export subsidies. India has consistently advocated for a permanent solution on public stockholding for food security, as the current provisions threaten to classify India's Minimum Support Price (MSP) procurement as trade-distorting under the Aggregate Measurement of Support (AMS) limits.

The TRIPS Agreement (Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights) sets minimum standards for IP protection across WTO members. India amended its Patents Act in 2005 to comply with TRIPS, introducing product patents in pharmaceuticals, while retaining Section 3(d) to prevent evergreening of patents, upheld in the landmark Novartis v. Union of India (2013) case by the Supreme Court.

The Most Favoured Nation (MFN) principle under Article I of GATT requires that any trade advantage granted by a WTO member to one country must be extended to all WTO members, ensuring non-discrimination. Exceptions include Regional Trade Agreements (RTAs) under Article XXIV and the Enabling Clause for developing countries, which allow preferential treatment through FTAs and the Generalized System of Preferences (GSP).

International Treaties on Outer Space.title: International Treaties on Outer Space
International Treaties on Outer Space.desc: International treaties on outer space constitute the legal regime governing the exploration and use of outer space, celestial bodies, and related activities, primarily developed under the auspices of the United Nations Committee on the Peaceful Uses of Outer Space (UNCOPUOS) established in 1959. The foundational treaty is the Outer Space Treaty of 1967, supplemented by four other UN space treaties covering rescue of astronauts, liability for damage, registration of space objects, and activities on the Moon. UPSC aspirants must study this topic for its relevance to India's space programme (ISRO), space diplomacy, the emerging space economy, militarization of space, and ethical questions around space debris and resource exploitation.
facts: The Outer Space Treaty (1967), formally the Treaty on Principles Governing the Activities of States in the Exploration and Use of Outer Space, is the magna carta of space law. It establishes that outer space is free for exploration by all states, cannot be subject to national appropriation, and prohibits the placement of nuclear weapons or weapons of mass destruction in orbit. India ratified this treaty in 1982.

The Liability Convention (1972) establishes that a launching state is absolutely liable for damage caused by its space objects on the surface of the Earth and liable based on fault for damage in outer space. The only instance of its invocation was when Canada claimed against the Soviet Union in 1978 for the crash of the nuclear-powered satellite Cosmos 954 in Canadian territory.

The Moon Agreement (1979) declares the Moon and its natural resources as the common heritage of mankind and calls for an international regime to govern resource exploitation. It has been ratified by only 18 states and notably not by any major space-faring nation including India, the USA, Russia, or China, rendering it largely ineffective.

India enacted the Indian Space Policy 2023 to open up the space sector to private participation, designating IN-SPACe (Indian National Space Promotion and Authorization Centre) as the single-window agency for authorizing space activities. India's successful Chandrayaan-3 mission (August 2023) made India the fourth country to land on the Moon and the first to land near the lunar south pole.

The growing concern over space debris — with over 36,000 tracked objects larger than 10 cm in orbit — has led to the development of the Inter-Agency Space Debris Coordination Committee (IADC) Guidelines and the UN Space Debris Mitigation Guidelines (2007). India's Anti-Satellite (ASAT) test (Mission Shakti, March 2019) generated debris but was conducted at a low altitude of 300 km to ensure rapid orbital decay and minimize long-term debris.

International conflict resolution mechan.title: International Conflict Resolution Mechanisms
International conflict resolution mechan.desc: International conflict resolution mechanisms comprise the legal, diplomatic, and institutional frameworks through which disputes between states are peacefully settled, as mandated by Article 2(3) and Chapter VI of the UN Charter. These mechanisms include negotiation, mediation, arbitration, judicial settlement through the International Court of Justice (ICJ), and collective security measures under the UN Security Council (Chapter VII). UPSC aspirants must understand these mechanisms for their relevance to India's foreign policy, its role in UN peacekeeping, its disputes with neighbouring countries, and the broader theme of rules-based international order.
facts: Article 33 of the UN Charter enumerates the primary means of peaceful dispute settlement: negotiation, enquiry, mediation, conciliation, arbitration, judicial settlement, and resort to regional agencies or arrangements. The parties to any dispute whose continuance is likely to endanger international peace and security are obligated to seek a solution by these means first.

The International Court of Justice (ICJ), established in 1945 as the principal judicial organ of the United Nations and seated in The Hague, Netherlands, has jurisdiction over contentious cases between states and renders advisory opinions. India was a party in several ICJ cases, most notably the Jadhav Case (India v. Pakistan, 2019) where the ICJ ruled that Pakistan had violated the Vienna Convention on Consular Relations by denying consular access.

The Permanent Court of Arbitration (PCA), established by the Hague Convention of 1899, provides a framework for arbitration and other forms of dispute resolution. The South China Sea Arbitration (Philippines v. China, 2016) under UNCLOS resulted in a landmark ruling against China's nine-dash line claims, which China refused to accept, highlighting the limitations of enforcement in international law.

India has been one of the largest contributors to UN Peacekeeping Operations, having deployed over 275,000 troops across 49 missions since 1950. India's first major peacekeeping contribution was to the UN Operation in Korea (1950), and it has served prominently in missions in Congo, Cambodia, Somalia, Rwanda, and South Sudan, with over 175 Indian peacekeepers having made the ultimate sacrifice.

The UN Security Council, with five permanent members (P5) wielding veto power, can authorize binding resolutions, sanctions, and the use of force under Chapter VII of the UN Charter. India has been a non-permanent member eight times, most recently in 2021-22, and has been a vocal advocate for UNSC reform, seeking permanent membership through the G4 grouping with Germany, Japan, and Brazil.

Investment Models.title: Investment Models
Investment Models.desc: Investment models refer to the various frameworks and structures through which public and private capital is channelled into infrastructure, industry, and development projects in India, including Public-Private Partnerships (PPP), Build-Operate-Transfer (BOT), Hybrid Annuity Model (HAM), and Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) routes. These models are governed by frameworks like the NITI Aayog's PPP guidelines, the FDI Policy under FEMA, and sector-specific regulations. UPSC aspirants must understand investment models as they are critical to questions on infrastructure development, economic reforms, fiscal policy, and the mobilization of resources for India's development needs.
facts: The Public-Private Partnership (PPP) model in India is facilitated by the PPP Appraisal Committee (PPPAC) under the Department of Economic Affairs and the Viability Gap Funding (VGF) scheme which provides capital grants of up to 20% of Total Project Cost (TPC) from the central government to make commercially unviable projects bankable. India has one of the world's largest PPP programmes with over 1,900 projects.

The Hybrid Annuity Model (HAM), introduced in 2016 for highway projects under NHAI, combines features of BOT-Annuity and EPC models. The government contributes 40% of the project cost during the construction phase in five equal instalments, while the private developer raises the remaining 60% and recovers it through annuity payments over the concession period.

India's Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) policy operates through two routes: the Automatic Route (no prior government approval needed) and the Government Route (approval through the concerned ministry). India attracted record FDI inflows of $84.8 billion in FY 2021-22, with the service sector, computer software and hardware, and telecommunications being the top recipient sectors.

The National Infrastructure Pipeline (NIP), launched in 2019, projected total infrastructure investment of Rs 111 lakh crore ($1.5 trillion) during FY 2020-2025, with the Centre expected to contribute 39%, states 40%, and the private sector 21%. The National Monetization Pipeline (NMP) announced in 2021 targets monetization of Rs 6 lakh crore worth of brownfield public assets.

The Build-Operate-Transfer (BOT) model operates in two variants: BOT-Toll, where the private developer collects user charges and bears demand risk, and BOT-Annuity, where the government makes fixed annuity payments irrespective of traffic volume. The shift from BOT to HAM and EPC models in highway construction after 2014 was driven by the difficulty in attracting private investment due to high risk and stressed bank lending.

Issues Related to Direct and Indirect Fa.title: Issues Related to Direct and Indirect Farm Subsidies
Issues Related to Direct and Indirect Fa.desc: Farm subsidies in India encompass both direct transfers (PM-KISAN, crop insurance premium subsidies) and indirect subsidies (fertilizer subsidies, power subsidies, irrigation subsidies, and MSP-based procurement) aimed at supporting agricultural income and ensuring food security. These are governed by various policies including the National Food Security Act 2013, the Fertilizer Subsidy regime under the New Urea Policy, and are subject to WTO disciplines under the Agreement on Agriculture. UPSC aspirants must study this topic as it involves debates around fiscal sustainability, targeting efficiency, environmental impact, WTO compliance, and the broader question of agrarian distress and reform.
facts: The PM-KISAN (Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samman Nidhi) scheme, launched on February 24, 2019, provides direct income support of Rs 6,000 per year in three instalments to all landholding farmer families. As of 2024, it has disbursed over Rs 3 lakh crore to approximately 11 crore farmer families, making it one of the world's largest direct benefit transfer programmes.

India's fertilizer subsidy bill surged to a record Rs 2.55 lakh crore in FY 2022-23, driven by global price spikes following the Russia-Ukraine conflict. The subsidy operates through the Nutrient Based Subsidy (NBS) scheme for non-urea fertilizers and statutory price control for urea under the New Urea Policy 2015, which has been criticized for creating a nitrogen-phosphorus-potassium (N-P-K) use imbalance.

The WTO Agreement on Agriculture classifies domestic support into Green Box (non-trade distorting), Blue Box (production-limiting), and Amber Box (trade-distorting) categories. India's MSP-based procurement is classified under Amber Box support, and the Bali Ministerial Decision (2013) provides an interim peace clause protecting developing countries from legal challenges for exceeding the de minimis limit of 10% of the value of production.

The Shanta Kumar Committee (2015) on restructuring the Food Corporation of India recommended direct cash transfer of food subsidy to beneficiaries, limiting MSP procurement to select states, and reducing the number of beneficiaries under NFSA. It highlighted that only 6% of Indian farmers benefit from the MSP regime, indicating the highly skewed nature of indirect subsidies.

The Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY), launched in 2016, replaced earlier crop insurance schemes and operates on an actuarial premium basis with farmer premiums capped at 2% for Kharif, 1.5% for Rabi, and 5% for commercial crops, with the balance shared equally by Centre and state governments. It was made voluntary for all farmers from Kharif 2020 and covers over 4 crore farmer applications annually.

Issues of Buffer Stocks and Food Securit.title: Issues of Buffer Stocks and Food Security
Issues of Buffer Stocks and Food Securit.desc: Buffer stocks refer to the reserves of foodgrains (primarily wheat and rice) maintained by the Government of India through the Food Corporation of India (FCI) to ensure food security, stabilize prices, and meet the requirements of the Public Distribution System (PDS) and welfare programmes. The legal framework is anchored in the National Food Security Act (NFSA), 2013, and the buffer stocking norms are set by the central government. UPSC aspirants must study this topic because it connects food security with fiscal policy, WTO obligations on public stockholding, agricultural pricing, and the larger goal of zero hunger under SDG 2.
facts: The National Food Security Act (NFSA), 2013 provides a legal entitlement to 75% of the rural population and 50% of the urban population to receive subsidized foodgrains, covering approximately 81.35 crore persons. Under the Act, beneficiaries receive 5 kg per person per month of rice at Rs 3/kg, wheat at Rs 2/kg, and coarse grains at Rs 1/kg, while Antyodaya households receive 35 kg per household.

The FCI's buffer stocking norms prescribe minimum stock levels at the beginning of each quarter: for example, 21.04 million tonnes on July 1 (post-Rabi procurement) and 11.58 million tonnes on January 1. India's actual stocks have consistently exceeded buffer norms — in July 2024, central pool stocks stood at over 60 million tonnes, raising concerns about high carrying costs estimated at Rs 8-9 per kg.

The Shanta Kumar Committee (2015) estimated the economic cost of wheat and rice procured by FCI to be significantly higher than market prices due to procurement incidentals, storage losses, and transportation costs. It recommended outsourcing storage to the private sector, decentralized procurement, and liquidation of excess stocks through Open Market Sale Scheme (OMSS).

During the COVID-19 pandemic, the government launched the Pradhan Mantri Garib Kalyan Anna Yojana (PMGKAY) in March 2020, providing 5 kg of additional free foodgrains per person per month to NFSA beneficiaries. This scheme was extended multiple times and was subsumed into NFSA from January 2024, with the government providing free grains to all NFSA beneficiaries for five years until December 2028.

At the WTO, India faces challenges regarding its public stockholding programmes which may exceed the Aggregate Measurement of Support (AMS) de minimis limit of 10% of the value of production. The Bali Peace Clause (2013) provides interim protection, but India and the G-33 group of developing nations continue to push for a permanent solution, arguing that food security programmes in developing countries should be exempt from subsidy disciplines.
